name: Current Affairs in History & National Movement (10): Opening Monument Conservation to Private Agencies

sourceRefs.label: swarajyamag.com/pib.gov.in - Ministry of Culture opening monument conservation to private agencies (2026); ASI's 3,686 protected monuments; conservation tech and funding figures

researchNotes: Eleventh History current-affairs topic (10th lap). A significant policy shift in how India's heritage monuments are conserved — ending ASI's decades-long exclusive control — with concrete supporting facts (monument count, funding, technology).

reading.intro: Covers a major 2026 policy shift: India's Ministry of Culture opening monument conservation to private agencies, ending ASI's exclusive control.

qa.question: What major policy change did India's Ministry of Culture announce regarding monument conservation in 2026?
qa.answer: Allowing PRIVATE conservation agencies to participate in restoring the country's protected monuments — ending the Archaeological Survey of India's (ASI) longstanding exclusive control over this work.

qa.question: How can corporate donors participate in monument conservation under this new approach?
qa.answer: Through Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) contributions to the National Culture Fund — donors can choose from a list of 250 monuments urgently needing conservation, or approach the Ministry separately for monuments in specific regions.

qa.question: How many centrally protected monuments does the ASI currently safeguard, and what was conservation expenditure for 2024-25?
qa.answer: 3,686 centrally protected monuments; conservation expenditure for 2024-25 was ₹374 crores.

qa.question: What technologies are being integrated into India's monument conservation efforts?
qa.answer: LiDAR, GIS mapping, drones, 3D scanning, photogrammetry, and AI — used for documentation and diagnostics, including drone-based surveys conducted in collaboration with institutions like NISER (National Institute of Science Education and Research).

questions.prompt: India's Ministry of Culture's 2026 policy change on monument conservation primarily:
options: Opened conservation work to private agencies, ending ASI's exclusive control
Transferred all monuments to state governments
questions.explanation: Opened conservation to private agencies, ending ASI's exclusive control.
